# Business Glossary - Advanced Healthcare Management System ## Patient Management **Patient** An individual receiving medical care, treatment, or services from the healthcare organization. Each patient has a unique medical record number and may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods. Patients can be identified by their medical record number (MRN), which is distinct from any external insurance identifiers. **Patient Registration** The process of capturing essential demographic and administrative information when a patient first presents to the healthcare system. This includes personal details, emergency contacts, insurance information, and consent forms. ## Clinical Concepts **Diagnosis** The identification of a disease, condition, or injury based on clinical evaluation, laboratory results, and diagnostic imaging. Diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification) for billing and epidemiological purposes. A patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses with varying levels of clinical priority. **Principal Diagnosis** The primary condition identified after study as chiefly responsible for admission of the patient to the healthcare facility. This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.

## Financial & Administrative **Insurance** A third-party payer contract that provides coverage for medical services. Insurance types include private commercial plans, Medicare (federal), Medicaid (state), and TRICARE (military). Each insurance plan has specific coverage policies, prior authorization requirements, and cost-sharing structures. **Prior Authorization** The process of obtaining advance approval from an insurance payer before certain services, treatments, or medications can be provided. Authorization requirements vary by payer, procedure, and medical necessity criteria. **Claim** A formal request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services rendered to an insured patient. Claims include procedure codes (CPT/HCPCS), diagnosis codes (ICD-10), modifiers, and charges. Claims may be submitted on a fee-for-service or bundled payment basis. **Explanation of Benefits (EOB)** A document from the insurance payer explaining what services were covered, what portion was paid to the provider, and what financial responsibility remains for the patient (deductibles, copayments, coinsurance). **Coordination of Benefits (COB)** The process of determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient is covered by multiple payers. Rules are governed by state law and contract terms. ## Scheduling & Access **Appointment** A scheduled encounter between a patient and a provider for clinical services. Appointments can be in-person office visits, telehealth consultations, or procedural sessions. Status types include scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. **Appointment Type** The classification of an appointment based on purpose, duration, and resource requirements. Types include new patient evaluation, established patient follow-up, annual wellness visit, procedure, and imaging. **Patient Access** The functional area responsible for scheduling, registration, insurance verification, pre-certification, and financial counseling. Patient Access departments are often the first point of contact for patients entering the healthcare system. ## Clinical Workflows **Clinical Documentation** The creation and maintenance of electronic health record entries reflecting patient care, clinical decision-making, and treatment plans. Documentation must meet regulatory standards for completeness, accuracy, and timeliness. **Care Coordination** The deliberate organization of patient care activities between multiple participants involved in a patient's care to facilitate appropriate delivery of healthcare services. Coordination includes transitions of care, specialist referrals, and post-acute planning. **Transitions of Care** The movement of patients between healthcare settings, locations, or levels of care. Critical transition points include admission, transfer between units, discharge to post-acute facilities, and home health referral. ## Data Quality & Integrity **Data Governance** The policies, processes, and standards ensuring high-quality data throughout its lifecycle. Governance includes data stewardship, quality metrics, privacy protocols, and audit trails. **Historization** The practice of maintaining time-bounded versions of data records to capture state changes over time. Effective dating (valid_from, valid_to) allows reconstruction of historical states for auditing and clinical review. **Soft Delete** A logical deletion mechanism where records are marked as inactive rather than physically removed. Soft deletes preserve audit trails and allow recovery of accidentally deleted records. ## Regulatory & Privacy **HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)** Federal legislation regulating the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI). HIPAA establishes national standards for privacy, security, and breach notification. **Protected Health Information (PHI)** Individually identifiable health information transmitted or maintained in electronic, paper, or oral forms. PHI includes demographic data, medical histories, test results, and insurance information. **Minimum Necessary Standard** The HIPAA principle that only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to accomplish the intended purpose should be used, disclosed, or requested. **Patient Privacy Rights** Under HIPAA, patients have rights to access their medical records, request amendments, obtain an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of their information.
per_question.expected_answer: Join patients to treatments on patient_id, treatments to providers on provider_id, and providers to departments on department_id. Filter where department_name = 'Cardiology' (or department_code). Return patient MRN, name, treatment_name, treatment_date, and provider name. Exclude soft-deleted records from all tables. This query supports departmental workload analysis.

per_question.question: What treatments have been performed for a patient's specific diagnosis?
per_question.answer:
The retrieved context does not contain patient-level records, so it cannot determine which treatments have actually been performed for a specific patient’s diagnosis.

What is known from the context is the schema path to answer that question in the database. The relevant tables are DIAGNOSES and TREATMENTS. DIAGNOSES stores the patient diagnosis record, including diagnosis_id, patient_id, icd_10_code, diagnosis_name, diagnosis_type, provider_id, diagnosis_date, and resolution_date. TREATMENTS stores treatments and procedures performed on patients, including treatment_id, patient_id, diagnosis_id, treatment_name, cpt_code, provider_id, department_id, treatment_date, treatment_status, notes, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, and updated_at.

To find treatments for a patient’s specific diagnosis, the schema indicates you would identify the diagnosis in DIAGNOSES for that patient, then join TREATMENTS on TREATMENTS.diagnosis_id = DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_id. Because both tables also contain patient_id, the patient can be constrained there as well. The fields that would describe the treatments are treatment_name, cpt_code, treatment_date, treatment_status, provider_id, department_id, and notes. The TREATMENTS table explicitly describes diagnosis_id as the “Primary diagnosis indication for treatment.”

If the diagnosis is known by code or description rather than diagnosis_id, the context supports locating it in DIAGNOSES using icd_10_code and diagnosis_name first, then following the same join to TREATMENTS.

The context does not provide any actual treatment instances, so it cannot say which specific treatments were performed for any individual patient or diagnosis.

per_question.question: Which providers have prescribed medications to patients with a specific diagnosis?
per_question.answer:
The retrieved context does not contain operational patient/provider records, so it cannot tell which specific providers have prescribed medications to patients with a specific diagnosis.

What is known from the context is the schema needed to answer that question at the metadata level. The relevant path is DIAGNOSES → MEDICATIONS → PROVIDERS. DIAGNOSES stores patient diagnosis records and includes patient_id, icd_10_code, diagnosis_name, diagnosis_type, provider_id, and diagnosis_date. MEDICATIONS stores patient medication prescriptions/administrations and includes patient_id and prescribing_provider_id. PROVIDERS stores provider details and includes provider_id, npi, first_name, last_name, provider_type, specialty, and department_id.

Based on that schema, you would identify prescribing providers for patients with a given diagnosis by matching DIAGNOSES.patient_id to MEDICATIONS.patient_id, then matching MEDICATIONS.prescribing_provider_id to PROVIDERS.provider_id. The diagnosis could be filtered using DIAGNOSES.icd_10_code or DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_name, and DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_type could further distinguish principal, comorbidity, admitting, or secondary diagnoses.

A key limitation in the context is that MEDICATIONS has no diagnosis_id or other direct link to DIAGNOSES. So from the schema provided, you can find providers who prescribed medications to patients who have a specific diagnosis, but you cannot determine that a given medication was prescribed specifically for that diagnosis.

# Business Glossary - Advanced Healthcare Management System ## Patient Management **Patient** An individual receiving medical care, treatment, or services from the healthcare organization. Each patient has a unique medical record number and may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods. Patients can be identified by their medical record number (MRN), which is distinct from any external insurance identifiers. **Patient Registration** The process of capturing essential demographic and administrative information when a patient first presents to the healthcare system. This includes personal details, emergency contacts, insurance information, and consent forms. ## Clinical Concepts **Diagnosis** The identification of a disease, condition, or injury based on clinical evaluation, laboratory results, and diagnostic imaging. Diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification) for billing and epidemiological purposes. A patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses with varying levels of clinical priority. **Principal Diagnosis** The primary condition identified after study as chiefly responsible for admission of the patient to the healthcare facility. This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.

## Financial & Administrative **Insurance** A third-party payer contract that provides coverage for medical services. Insurance types include private commercial plans, Medicare (federal), Medicaid (state), and TRICARE (military). Each insurance plan has specific coverage policies, prior authorization requirements, and cost-sharing structures. **Prior Authorization** The process of obtaining advance approval from an insurance payer before certain services, treatments, or medications can be provided. Authorization requirements vary by payer, procedure, and medical necessity criteria. **Claim** A formal request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services rendered to an insured patient. Claims include procedure codes (CPT/HCPCS), diagnosis codes (ICD-10), modifiers, and charges. Claims may be submitted on a fee-for-service or bundled payment basis. **Explanation of Benefits (EOB)** A document from the insurance payer explaining what services were covered, what portion was paid to the provider, and what financial responsibility remains for the patient (deductibles, copayments, coinsurance). **Coordination of Benefits (COB)** The process of determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient is covered by multiple payers. Rules are governed by state law and contract terms. ## Scheduling & Access **Appointment** A scheduled encounter between a patient and a provider for clinical services. Appointments can be in-person office visits, telehealth consultations, or procedural sessions. Status types include scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. **Appointment Type** The classification of an appointment based on purpose, duration, and resource requirements. Types include new patient evaluation, established patient follow-up, annual wellness visit, procedure, and imaging. **Patient Access** The functional area responsible for scheduling, registration, insurance verification, pre-certification, and financial counseling. Patient Access departments are often the first point of contact for patients entering the healthcare system. ## Clinical Workflows **Clinical Documentation** The creation and maintenance of electronic health record entries reflecting patient care, clinical decision-making, and treatment plans. Documentation must meet regulatory standards for completeness, accuracy, and timeliness. **Care Coordination** The deliberate organization of patient care activities between multiple participants involved in a patient's care to facilitate appropriate delivery of healthcare services. Coordination includes transitions of care, specialist referrals, and post-acute planning. **Transitions of Care** The movement of patients between healthcare settings, locations, or levels of care. Critical transition points include admission, transfer between units, discharge to post-acute facilities, and home health referral. ## Data Quality & Integrity **Data Governance** The policies, processes, and standards ensuring high-quality data throughout its lifecycle. Governance includes data stewardship, quality metrics, privacy protocols, and audit trails. **Historization** The practice of maintaining time-bounded versions of data records to capture state changes over time. Effective dating (valid_from, valid_to) allows reconstruction of historical states for auditing and clinical review. **Soft Delete** A logical deletion mechanism where records are marked as inactive rather than physically removed. Soft deletes preserve audit trails and allow recovery of accidentally deleted records. ## Regulatory & Privacy **HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)** Federal legislation regulating the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI). HIPAA establishes national standards for privacy, security, and breach notification. **Protected Health Information (PHI)** Individually identifiable health information transmitted or maintained in electronic, paper, or oral forms. PHI includes demographic data, medical histories, test results, and insurance information. **Minimum Necessary Standard** The HIPAA principle that only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to accomplish the intended purpose should be used, disclosed, or requested. **Patient Privacy Rights** Under HIPAA, patients have rights to access their medical records, request amendments, obtain an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of their information.

# Data Dictionary - Advanced Healthcare Management System

## Database Overview
**Database Name**: `advanced_healthcare_db` **Schema Version**: `3.2.1` **Environment**: Production **Last Updated**: 2025-03-15 --- ## PATIENTS Table **Purpose**: Store current and historical patient demographic and administrative information. | Column Name | Data Type | Constraint | Description | |-------------|-----------|------------|-------------| | patient_id | INTEGER | PRIMARY KEY | Surrogate key for patient records | | mrn | VARCHAR(20) | UNIQUE NOT NULL | Medical Record Number - unique patient identifier | | first_name | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL | Patient's legal first name | | last_name | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL | Patient's legal last name | | date_of_birth | DATE | NOT NULL | Patient's date of birth for age calculation | | gender | VARCHAR(10) | NOT NULL, CHECK IN ('M', 'F', 'O', 'U') | Gender: Male

## Billing & Revenue Cycle **Charge Capture** The process of recording services and procedures for billing purposes. Charges must be captured at the time of service and linked to appropriate billing codes. **Claim Scrubbing** Automated validation of claims against payer-specific rules, coding guidelines, and regulatory requirements before submission. Scrubbing reduces claim rejections and denials. **Denial Management** The systematic process of tracking, analyzing, and appealing claim denials from payers. Effective denial management identifies root causes and implements corrective actions. **Revenue Cycle Management** The administrative and clinical functions that contribute to the capture, management, and collection of patient service revenue. The cycle spans from patient scheduling through final payment collection. ## Quality & Performance **Clinical Quality Measures** Standardized metrics assessing the quality of care delivered across specific conditions and procedures. Examples include readmission rates, mortality rates, complication rates, and adherence to evidence-based guidelines. **Value-Based Care** Payment models that reward healthcare providers for delivering high-quality, cost-efficient care rather than volume of services. Models include Accountable Care Organizations (ACOs) and bundled payments. **Population Health** The health outcomes of a group of individuals, including the distribution of such outcomes within the group. Population health management focuses on improving outcomes for defined patient panels through preventive care and chronic disease management.
per_question.expected_answer: Join diagnoses to patients on patient_id, patients to medications on patient_id, and medications to providers on prescribing_provider_id. Filter by specific icd_10_code. Return provider name, NPI, specialty, patient name, medication_name, dosage, and prescription dates. This supports medication pattern analysis for specific conditions.

per_question.question: What is the complete medication history for a patient including prescribing providers?
per_question.answer:
The knowledge graph contains schema-level metadata only, not actual patient-level medication records, so I cannot provide the medication history for a specific patient. From the available schema, the complete medication history would be retrieved from the MEDICATIONS table, which is the table for patient medication prescriptions and administrations.

To trace a patient’s full medication history, use MEDICATIONS and filter by MEDICATIONS.patient_id for the target patient. If you need to identify the patient from a medical record number, first use PATIENTS, where mrn is the unique medical record number, and join PATIENTS.patient_id to MEDICATIONS.patient_id.

The medication history fields available in MEDICATIONS are medication_id, patient_id, medication_name, ndc_code, dosage, route, frequency, prescribing_provider_id, start_date, end_date, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, and updated_at. This supports both the clinical medication details and historical tracking. Because the model includes valid_from and valid_to, it supports historization over time, and is_deleted indicates soft delete status, so a “complete” history should consider current and historical versions rather than only active rows.

To include prescribing providers, join MEDICATIONS.prescribing_provider_id to PROVIDERS.provider_id. From PROVIDERS, the available provider details are provider_id, npi, first_name, last_name, provider_type, specialty, department_id, and is_active. That join gives the prescribing provider attached to each medication record.

So, at the schema level, the path is PATIENTS.patient_id → MEDICATIONS.patient_id, and MEDICATIONS.prescribing_provider_id → PROVIDERS.provider_id. The resulting medication history can include the patient’s medications with medication_name, ndc_code, dosage, route, frequency, start_date, end_date, and the prescribing provider’s first_name, last_name, npi, provider_type, and specialty. The allowed route values in MEDICATIONS are oral, IV, IM, topical, inhaled, and subcutaneous.

per_question.question: Which departments have the highest volume of patient appointments?
per_question.answer:
This cannot be determined from the retrieved context because it contains schema and business glossary information, not appointment record counts.

What is known from the context is that patient appointments are stored in the APPOINTMENTS table, and each appointment has a department_id that links to the DEPARTMENTS table via department_id. The APPOINTMENTS table is described as storing patient appointment scheduling and encounter information, with fields including appointment_id, patient_id, provider_id, department_id, appointment_date, appointment_time, appointment_type, and appointment_status. The context also states that the DEPARTMENTS table exists and contains department identifiers, names, codes, hierarchy, service line, and location.

So, at the schema level, the way to determine which departments have the highest volume of patient appointments would be to count APPOINTMENTS.appointment_id grouped by APPOINTMENTS.department_id, and join to DEPARTMENTS.department_id to retrieve department details such as the department name. However, the retrieved context does not include the actual appointment data or any aggregated counts, so it does not show which departments have the highest volume.

Authorization requirements vary by payer, procedure, and medical necessity criteria. **Claim** A formal request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services rendered to an insured patient. Claims may be submitted on a fee-for-service or bundled payment basis. **Explanation of Benefits (EOB)** A document from the insurance payer explaining what services were covered, what portion was paid to the provider, and what financial responsibility remains for the patient (deductibles, copayments, coinsurance). **Coordination of Benefits (COB)** The process of determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient is covered by multiple payers. Rules are governed by state law and contract terms. ## Scheduling & Access **Appointment** A scheduled encounter between a patient and a provider for clinical services. Appointments can be in-person office visits, telehealth consultations, or procedural sessions. Status types include scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. **Appointment Type** The classification of an appointment based on purpose, duration, and resource requirements. Types include new patient evaluation, established patient follow-up, annual wellness visit, procedure, and imaging. **Patient Access** The functional area responsible for scheduling, registration, insurance verification, pre-certification, and financial counseling. Patient Access departments are often the first point of contact for patients entering the healthcare system. ## Clinical Workflows **Clinical Documentation** The creation and maintenance of electronic health record entries reflecting patient care, clinical decision-making, and treatment plans. Documentation must meet regulatory standards for completeness, accuracy, and timeliness. **Care Coordination** The deliberate organization of patient care activities between multiple participants involved in a patient's care to facilitate appropriate delivery of healthcare services. Coordination includes transitions of care, specialist referrals, and post-acute planning. **Transitions of Care** The movement of patients between healthcare settings, locations, or levels of care. Critical transition points include admission, transfer between units, discharge to post-acute facilities, and home health referral. ## Data Quality & Integrity **Data Governance** The policies, processes, and standards ensuring high-quality data throughout its lifecycle. Governance includes data stewardship, quality metrics, privacy protocols, and audit trails. **Historization** The practice of maintaining time-bounded versions of data records to capture state changes over time. Effective dating (valid_from, valid_to) allows reconstruction of historical states for auditing and clinical review. **Soft Delete** A logical deletion mechanism where records are marked as inactive rather than physically removed. Soft deletes preserve audit trails and allow recovery of accidentally deleted records. ## Regulatory & Privacy **HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)** Federal legislation regulating the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI). HIPAA establishes national standards for privacy, security, and breach notification. **Protected Health Information (PHI)** Individually identifiable health information transmitted or maintained in electronic, paper, or oral forms. PHI includes demographic data, medical histories, test results, and insurance information. **Minimum Necessary Standard** The HIPAA principle that only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to accomplish the intended purpose should be used, disclosed, or requested. **Patient Privacy Rights** Under HIPAA, patients have rights to access their medical records, request amendments, obtain an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of their information.

# Business Glossary - Advanced Healthcare Management System ## Patient Management **Patient** An individual receiving medical care, treatment, or services from the healthcare organization. Each patient has a unique medical record number and may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods. Patients can be identified by their medical record number (MRN), which is distinct from any external insurance identifiers. **Patient Registration** The process of capturing essential demographic and administrative information when a patient first presents to the healthcare system. This includes personal details, emergency contacts, insurance information, and consent forms. ## Clinical Concepts **Diagnosis** The identification of a disease, condition, or injury based on clinical evaluation, laboratory results, and diagnostic imaging. Diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification) for billing and epidemiological purposes. A patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses with varying levels of clinical priority. **Principal Diagnosis** The primary condition identified after study as chiefly responsible for admission of the patient to the healthcare facility. This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.

## Billing & Revenue Cycle **Charge Capture** The process of recording services and procedures for billing purposes. Charges must be captured at the time of service and linked to appropriate billing codes. **Claim Scrubbing** Automated validation of claims against payer-specific rules, coding guidelines, and regulatory requirements before submission. Scrubbing reduces claim rejections and denials. **Denial Management** The systematic process of tracking, analyzing, and appealing claim denials from payers. Effective denial management identifies root causes and implements corrective actions. **Revenue Cycle Management** The administrative and clinical functions that contribute to the capture, management, and collection of patient service revenue. The cycle spans from patient scheduling through final payment collection. ## Quality & Performance **Clinical Quality Measures** Standardized metrics assessing the quality of care delivered across specific conditions and procedures. Examples include readmission rates, mortality rates, complication rates, and adherence to evidence-based guidelines. **Value-Based Care** Payment models that reward healthcare providers for delivering high-quality, cost-efficient care rather than volume of services. Models include Accountable Care Organizations (ACOs) and bundled payments. **Population Health** The health outcomes of a group of individuals, including the distribution of such outcomes within the group. Population health management focuses on improving outcomes for defined patient panels through preventive care and chronic disease management.

per_question.question: What claims have been submitted for a specific patient's treatments?
per_question.answer:
I cannot identify the actual claims for a specific patient from the knowledge graph because it contains schema and business metadata only, not operational claim records.

From the schema, submitted claims are stored in the CLAIMS table, which implements the Claims business concept and stores claim records for services rendered and billing outcomes. Relevant CLAIMS columns are claim_id, patient_id, insurance_plan_id, claim_number, service_date, submission_date, cpt_code, icd_10_code, amount_charged, amount_allowed, amount_paid, claim_status, denial_reason, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, and updated_at. Because submission_date is defined as NOT NULL, each CLAIMS record represents a claim that has been submitted.

To trace submitted claims for a patient’s treatments at the schema level, use PATIENTS.patient_id = TREATMENTS.patient_id and PATIENTS.patient_id = CLAIMS.patient_id. To connect a claim to a specific treatment, the shared billing/procedure field is TREATMENTS.cpt_code and CLAIMS.cpt_code. To connect through the treatment’s diagnosis, use TREATMENTS.diagnosis_id = DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_id, then compare DIAGNOSES.icd_10_code to CLAIMS.icd_10_code, since CLAIMS.icd_10_code is the diagnosis code supporting medical necessity.

So the schema-level answer is: for a given patient, the relevant submitted claims are the rows in CLAIMS for that patient, and they can be linked to that patient’s treatments by patient_id and, more specifically, by matching cpt_code, with diagnosis support traceable through DIAGNOSES.icd_10_code. The specific output fields that describe those claims would be claim_number, service_date, submission_date, cpt_code, icd_10_code, claim_status, denial_reason, amount_charged, amount_allowed, and amount_paid.

per_question.question: Which providers in a specific department have ordered abnormal lab results?
per_question.answer:
The knowledge graph does not contain actual provider or lab result records, so it cannot tell you which specific providers have ordered abnormal lab results in a given department. It contains schema-level metadata only.

From the schema, the relevant path is LAB_RESULTS → PROVIDERS using LAB_RESULTS.ordering_provider_id = PROVIDERS.provider_id. To restrict to abnormal results, use LAB_RESULTS.is_abnormal, which is a boolean flag for results outside the reference range. To restrict to a specific department, use PROVIDERS.department_id, since the PROVIDERS table stores each provider’s primary department affiliation as department_id.

The relevant fields available are:
From LAB_RESULTS: lab_result_id, patient_id, test_name, loinc_code, test_value, unit, reference_range, is_abnormal, ordering_provider_id, result_date, notes, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, updated_at.
From PROVIDERS: provider_id, npi, first_name, last_name, provider_type, specialty, department_id, is_active, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, updated_at.

So at the schema level, the providers you would identify are those in PROVIDERS where department_id matches the target department and whose provider_id appears in LAB_RESULTS.ordering_provider_id for rows where is_abnormal = TRUE. If you need the department name rather than department_id, that cannot be answered from the provided context because the DEPARTMENTS table definition is not included.

# Business Glossary - Advanced Healthcare Management System ## Patient Management **Patient** An individual receiving medical care, treatment, or services from the healthcare organization. Each patient has a unique medical record number and may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods. Patients can be identified by their medical record number (MRN), which is distinct from any external insurance identifiers. **Patient Registration** The process of capturing essential demographic and administrative information when a patient first presents to the healthcare system. This includes personal details, emergency contacts, insurance information, and consent forms. ## Clinical Concepts **Diagnosis** The identification of a disease, condition, or injury based on clinical evaluation, laboratory results, and diagnostic imaging. Diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification) for billing and epidemiological purposes. A patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses with varying levels of clinical priority. **Principal Diagnosis** The primary condition identified after study as chiefly responsible for admission of the patient to the healthcare facility. This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.

## Financial & Administrative **Insurance** A third-party payer contract that provides coverage for medical services. Insurance types include private commercial plans, Medicare (federal), Medicaid (state), and TRICARE (military). Each insurance plan has specific coverage policies, prior authorization requirements, and cost-sharing structures. **Prior Authorization** The process of obtaining advance approval from an insurance payer before certain services, treatments, or medications can be provided. Authorization requirements vary by payer, procedure, and medical necessity criteria. **Claim** A formal request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services rendered to an insured patient. Claims include procedure codes (CPT/HCPCS), diagnosis codes (ICD-10), modifiers, and charges. Claims may be submitted on a fee-for-service or bundled payment basis. **Explanation of Benefits (EOB)** A document from the insurance payer explaining what services were covered, what portion was paid to the provider, and what financial responsibility remains for the patient (deductibles, copayments, coinsurance). **Coordination of Benefits (COB)** The process of determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient is covered by multiple payers. Rules are governed by state law and contract terms. ## Scheduling & Access **Appointment** A scheduled encounter between a patient and a provider for clinical services. Appointments can be in-person office visits, telehealth consultations, or procedural sessions. Status types include scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. **Appointment Type** The classification of an appointment based on purpose, duration, and resource requirements. Types include new patient evaluation, established patient follow-up, annual wellness visit, procedure, and imaging. **Patient Access** The functional area responsible for scheduling, registration, insurance verification, pre-certification, and financial counseling. Patient Access departments are often the first point of contact for patients entering the healthcare system. ## Clinical Workflows **Clinical Documentation** The creation and maintenance of electronic health record entries reflecting patient care, clinical decision-making, and treatment plans. Documentation must meet regulatory standards for completeness, accuracy, and timeliness. **Care Coordination** The deliberate organization of patient care activities between multiple participants involved in a patient's care to facilitate appropriate delivery of healthcare services. Coordination includes transitions of care, specialist referrals, and post-acute planning. **Transitions of Care** The movement of patients between healthcare settings, locations, or levels of care. Critical transition points include admission, transfer between units, discharge to post-acute facilities, and home health referral. ## Data Quality & Integrity **Data Governance** The policies, processes, and standards ensuring high-quality data throughout its lifecycle. Governance includes data stewardship, quality metrics, privacy protocols, and audit trails. **Historization** The practice of maintaining time-bounded versions of data records to capture state changes over time. Effective dating (valid_from, valid_to) allows reconstruction of historical states for auditing and clinical review. **Soft Delete** A logical deletion mechanism where records are marked as inactive rather than physically removed. Soft deletes preserve audit trails and allow recovery of accidentally deleted records. ## Regulatory & Privacy **HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)** Federal legislation regulating the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI). HIPAA establishes national standards for privacy, security, and breach notification. **Protected Health Information (PHI)** Individually identifiable health information transmitted or maintained in electronic, paper, or oral forms. PHI includes demographic data, medical histories, test results, and insurance information. **Minimum Necessary Standard** The HIPAA principle that only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to accomplish the intended purpose should be used, disclosed, or requested. **Patient Privacy Rights** Under HIPAA, patients have rights to access their medical records, request amendments, obtain an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of their information.

## MEDICATIONS Table
**Purpose**: Store patient medication prescriptions and administrations. | Column Name | Data Type | Constraint | Description | |-------------|-----------|------------|-------------| | medication_id | INTEGER | PRIMARY KEY | Surrogate key for medication records | | patient_id | INTEGER | FK → patients.patient_id, NOT NULL | Patient receiving medication | | medication_name | VARCHAR(255) | NOT NULL | Generic or brand medication name | | ndc_code | VARCHAR(11) | National Drug Code for identification | | dosage | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL | Dosage amount (e.g., '10mg', '500mg') | | route | VARCHAR(20) | CHECK IN ('oral', 'IV', 'IM', 'topical', 'inhaled', 'subcutaneous') | Route of administration | | frequency | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL | Administration frequency (e.g., 'BID', 'TID', 'PRN') | | prescribing_prov

## Billing & Revenue Cycle **Charge Capture** The process of recording services and procedures for billing purposes. Charges must be captured at the time of service and linked to appropriate billing codes. **Claim Scrubbing** Automated validation of claims against payer-specific rules, coding guidelines, and regulatory requirements before submission. Scrubbing reduces claim rejections and denials. **Denial Management** The systematic process of tracking, analyzing, and appealing claim denials from payers. Effective denial management identifies root causes and implements corrective actions. **Revenue Cycle Management** The administrative and clinical functions that contribute to the capture, management, and collection of patient service revenue. The cycle spans from patient scheduling through final payment collection. ## Quality & Performance **Clinical Quality Measures** Standardized metrics assessing the quality of care delivered across specific conditions and procedures. Examples include readmission rates, mortality rates, complication rates, and adherence to evidence-based guidelines. **Value-Based Care** Payment models that reward healthcare providers for delivering high-quality, cost-efficient care rather than volume of services. Models include Accountable Care Organizations (ACOs) and bundled payments. **Population Health** The health outcomes of a group of individuals, including the distribution of such outcomes within the group. Population health management focuses on improving outcomes for defined patient panels through preventive care and chronic disease management.

per_question.question: What is a patient's complete care journey including diagnoses, treatments, and medications?
per_question.answer:
From the retrieved schema and glossary, a patient’s care journey can be traced at the schema level by linking the patient’s diagnoses, treatments, and medications through the shared patient identifier and the diagnosis-to-treatment relationship.

The core join path is:
PATIENTS.patient_id = DIAGNOSES.patient_id
PATIENTS.patient_id = TREATMENTS.patient_id
PATIENTS.patient_id = MEDICATIONS.patient_id
and, to connect a treatment to its clinical justification,
TREATMENTS.diagnosis_id = DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_id.

Using those tables together gives this view of the journey:

The patient identity anchor is PATIENTS, which stores demographic and administrative information and includes patient_id as the primary key and mrn as the unique medical record number.

DIAGNOSES represents the diagnosis portion of the journey. It stores diagnosis_id, patient_id, icd_10_code, diagnosis_name, diagnosis_type, provider_id, diagnosis_date, and resolution_date, plus is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, and updated_at. The glossary states that a diagnosis is the identification of a disease, condition, or injury, coded using ICD-10-CM, and that a patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses. The diagnosis_type field classifies diagnoses as principal, comorbidity, admitting, or secondary. This lets you identify the primary condition and any concurrent conditions over time.

TREATMENTS represents the intervention portion of the journey. It stores treatment_id, patient_id, diagnosis_id, treatment_name, cpt_code, provider_id, department_id, treatment_date, treatment_status, notes, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, and updated_at. The glossary defines treatment as any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition, and explicitly states that each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. In the schema, that linkage is implemented by TREATMENTS.diagnosis_id pointing to DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_id. This means you can trace which treatment or procedure was performed for which diagnosis, who performed it, in which department, on what date, and with what billing-oriented CPT/HCPCS code.

MEDICATIONS represents the medication portion of the journey. It stores medication_id, patient_id, medication_name, ndc_code, dosage, route, frequency, prescribing_provider_id, start_date, end_date, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, and updated_at. The glossary defines medication as a pharmaceutical substance administered for therapeutic purposes, and says each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. The route field is constrained to oral, IV, IM, topical, inhaled, or subcutaneous. This allows you to see what medication the patient received or was prescribed, in what amount, by what route, how often, who prescribed it, and during what time window.

To assemble a chronological care journey, use the date fields across these tables: diagnosis_date and resolution_date from DIAGNOSES, treatment_date from TREATMENTS, and start_date and end_date from MEDICATIONS. The historization fields valid_from and valid_to support reconstruction of historical states over time, and is_deleted indicates soft-deleted records that were logically inactivated rather than physically removed.

If you want a broader encounter context around that journey, APPOINTMENTS also exists and links to the same patient through patient_id, with provider_id, department_id, appointment_date, appointment_time, appointment_type, and status values such as scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. But for diagnoses, treatments, and medications specifically, the main path is PATIENTS → DIAGNOSES / TREATMENTS / MEDICATIONS, plus TREATMENTS → DIAGNOSES through diagnosis_id.

The retrieved context does not include actual patient records, so it cannot provide one specific patient’s complete care journey. It does provide the table structure and relationships needed to reconstruct that journey from the database.

per_question.question: Which insurance plans have the highest claim denial rates?
per_question.answer:
The retrieved context does not provide claim or plan-level data, so it cannot determine which specific insurance plans have the highest claim denial rates.

What is known from the context is the schema needed to calculate it. The `CLAIMS` table stores claim records and links each claim to an insurance plan through `insurance_plan_id`, which is a foreign key to `INSURANCE_PLANS.plan_id`. The claims concept also includes `claim_status` and `denial_reason`, and the insurance plan table includes `plan_name`, `payer_name`, `plan_type`, `prior_auth_required`, and `is_active`.

So, at a schema level, denial rates would be derived by joining `CLAIMS.insurance_plan_id = INSURANCE_PLANS.plan_id`, grouping by the plan fields you want to report on (for example `plan_name` or `payer_name`), counting total claims, counting denied claims based on `CLAIMS.claim_status`, and dividing denied claims by total claims. However, the context does not provide any actual records, counts, or even the specific `claim_status` values that indicate “denied,” so the highest-denial plans cannot be identified from the knowledge graph.

If you want only current, non-deleted records, the general conventions say queries should include `valid_to IS NULL` and `is_deleted = FALSE`.

It physically stores claim records for services rendered and associated billing outcomes. is implemented by physical table CLAIMS (columns: claim_id, patient_id, insurance_plan_id, claim_number, service_date, submission_date, cpt_code, icd_10_code, amount_charged, amount_allowed, amount_paid, claim_status, denial_reason, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, updated_at).

## General Conventions

### Temporal Validity Pattern
All major tables use `valid_from` and `valid_to` timestamps for historization: - Current records have `valid_to = NULL` - Historical records have non-NULL `valid_to` - Queries must include `WHERE valid_to IS NULL` to retrieve current data - Historical analysis possible by querying all records ### Soft Delete Pattern All tables include `is_deleted` flag: - `is_deleted = FALSE` for active records - `is_deleted = TRUE` for logically deleted records - Queries must include `WHERE is_deleted = FALSE` to exclude deleted records - Soft deletes preserve audit trail ### Audit Timestamps All tables include: - `created_at` - set once on INSERT - `updated_at` - updated on each UPDATE ### Naming Conventions - Table names: plural_snake_case (e.g., `insurance_plans`, `lab_results`) - Column names: snake_case - Foreign keys: `{table}_{column}` (e.g., `patient_id`, `provider_id`) - In

Business concept 'Insurance Plan' — The table is explicitly described as storing insurance payer and plan information, with fields for plan name, payer name, plan type, prior authorization, and validity dates. This aligns directly with an insurance plan master rather than a contract or explanation-of-benefits document. is implemented by physical table INSURANCE_PLANS (columns: plan_id, plan_name, payer_name, plan_type, prior_auth_required, is_active, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, updated_at).

## Financial & Administrative **Insurance** A third-party payer contract that provides coverage for medical services. Insurance types include private commercial plans, Medicare (federal), Medicaid (state), and TRICARE (military). Each insurance plan has specific coverage policies, prior authorization requirements, and cost-sharing structures. **Prior Authorization** The process of obtaining advance approval from an insurance payer before certain services, treatments, or medications can be provided. Authorization requirements vary by payer, procedure, and medical necessity criteria. **Claim** A formal request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services rendered to an insured patient. Claims include procedure codes (CPT/HCPCS), diagnosis codes (ICD-10), modifiers, and charges. Claims may be submitted on a fee-for-service or bundled payment basis. **Explanation of Benefits (EOB)** A document from the insurance payer explaining what services were covered, what portion was paid to the provider, and what financial responsibility remains for the patient (deductibles, copayments, coinsurance). **Coordination of Benefits (COB)** The process of determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient is covered by multiple payers. Rules are governed by state law and contract terms. ## Scheduling & Access **Appointment** A scheduled encounter between a patient and a provider for clinical services. Appointments can be in-person office visits, telehealth consultations, or procedural sessions. Status types include scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. **Appointment Type** The classification of an appointment based on purpose, duration, and resource requirements. Types include new patient evaluation, established patient follow-up, annual wellness visit, procedure, and imaging. **Patient Access** The functional area responsible for scheduling, registration, insurance verification, pre-certification, and financial counseling. Patient Access departments are often the first point of contact for patients entering the healthcare system. ## Clinical Workflows **Clinical Documentation** The creation and maintenance of electronic health record entries reflecting patient care, clinical decision-making, and treatment plans. Documentation must meet regulatory standards for completeness, accuracy, and timeliness. **Care Coordination** The deliberate organization of patient care activities between multiple participants involved in a patient's care to facilitate appropriate delivery of healthcare services. Coordination includes transitions of care, specialist referrals, and post-acute planning. **Transitions of Care** The movement of patients between healthcare settings, locations, or levels of care. Critical transition points include admission, transfer between units, discharge to post-acute facilities, and home health referral. ## Data Quality & Integrity **Data Governance** The policies, processes, and standards ensuring high-quality data throughout its lifecycle. Governance includes data stewardship, quality metrics, privacy protocols, and audit trails. **Historization** The practice of maintaining time-bounded versions of data records to capture state changes over time. Effective dating (valid_from, valid_to) allows reconstruction of historical states for auditing and clinical review. **Soft Delete** A logical deletion mechanism where records are marked as inactive rather than physically removed. Soft deletes preserve audit trails and allow recovery of accidentally deleted records. ## Regulatory & Privacy **HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)** Federal legislation regulating the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI). HIPAA establishes national standards for privacy, security, and breach notification. **Protected Health Information (PHI)** Individually identifiable health information transmitted or maintained in electronic, paper, or oral forms. PHI includes demographic data, medical histories, test results, and insurance information. **Minimum Necessary Standard** The HIPAA principle that only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to accomplish the intended purpose should be used, disclosed, or requested. **Patient Privacy Rights** Under HIPAA, patients have rights to access their medical records, request amendments, obtain an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of their information.

## Billing & Revenue Cycle **Charge Capture** The process of recording services and procedures for billing purposes. Charges must be captured at the time of service and linked to appropriate billing codes. **Claim Scrubbing** Automated validation of claims against payer-specific rules, coding guidelines, and regulatory requirements before submission. Scrubbing reduces claim rejections and denials. **Denial Management** The systematic process of tracking, analyzing, and appealing claim denials from payers. Effective denial management identifies root causes and implements corrective actions. **Revenue Cycle Management** The administrative and clinical functions that contribute to the capture, management, and collection of patient service revenue. The cycle spans from patient scheduling through final payment collection. ## Quality & Performance **Clinical Quality Measures** Standardized metrics assessing the quality of care delivered across specific conditions and procedures. Examples include readmission rates, mortality rates, complication rates, and adherence to evidence-based guidelines. **Value-Based Care** Payment models that reward healthcare providers for delivering high-quality, cost-efficient care rather than volume of services. Models include Accountable Care Organizations (ACOs) and bundled payments. **Population Health** The health outcomes of a group of individuals, including the distribution of such outcomes within the group. Population health management focuses on improving outcomes for defined patient panels through preventive care and chronic disease management.

# Data Dictionary - Advanced Healthcare Management System

## Database Overview
**Database Name**: `advanced_healthcare_db` **Schema Version**: `3.2.1` **Environment**: Production **Last Updated**: 2025-03-15 --- ## PATIENTS Table **Purpose**: Store current and historical patient demographic and administrative information. | Column Name | Data Type | Constraint | Description | |-------------|-----------|------------|-------------| | patient_id | INTEGER | PRIMARY KEY | Surrogate key for patient records | | mrn | VARCHAR(20) | UNIQUE NOT NULL | Medical Record Number - unique patient identifier | | first_name | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL | Patient's legal first name | | last_name | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL | Patient's legal last name | | date_of_birth | DATE | NOT NULL | Patient's date of birth for age calculation | | gender | VARCHAR(10) | NOT NULL, CHECK IN ('M', 'F', 'O', 'U') | Gender: Male

# Business Glossary - Advanced Healthcare Management System ## Patient Management **Patient** An individual receiving medical care, treatment, or services from the healthcare organization. Each patient has a unique medical record number and may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods. Patients can be identified by their medical record number (MRN), which is distinct from any external insurance identifiers. **Patient Registration** The process of capturing essential demographic and administrative information when a patient first presents to the healthcare system. This includes personal details, emergency contacts, insurance information, and consent forms. ## Clinical Concepts **Diagnosis** The identification of a disease, condition, or injury based on clinical evaluation, laboratory results, and diagnostic imaging. Diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification) for billing and epidemiological purposes. A patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses with varying levels of clinical priority. **Principal Diagnosis** The primary condition identified after study as chiefly responsible for admission of the patient to the healthcare facility. This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.

Claims: The table name, purpose comment, and columns such as claim_number, submission_date, claim_status, denial_reason, and billed/allowed/paid amounts align directly with the insurance claims domain. It physically stores claim records for services rendered and associated billing outcomes.

per_question.expected_answer: Join claims to insurance_plans on insurance_plan_id. Group by plan_id, payer_name, and plan_type. Count total claims and count claims where claim_status = 'denied'. per_question.question: What were a patient's diagnoses in a specific past time period?
per_question.answer:
The context does not provide actual patient diagnosis records, only the schema and business definitions. At the schema level, you would use the DIAGNOSES table to answer this.

DIAGNOSES stores patient diagnosis records with ICD-10 coding and temporal tracking. The patient is identified by DIAGNOSES.patient_id, which is a foreign key to PATIENTS.patient_id. The diagnosis details available are diagnosis_id, icd_10_code, diagnosis_name, diagnosis_type, provider_id, diagnosis_date, resolution_date, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, and updated_at.

For a specific past time period, the historical reconstruction should use valid_from and valid_to, because the glossary defines historization as maintaining time-bounded versions of records and says effective dating with valid_from and valid_to allows reconstruction of historical states. If you want diagnoses that were recorded during that period, diagnosis_date is also relevant because it is the diagnosis date column. If you want to avoid logically deleted records, use is_deleted, since the glossary defines soft delete as marking records inactive rather than physically removing them.

The diagnosis classifications available in DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_type are principal, comorbidity, admitting, and secondary. This aligns with the glossary, which defines Diagnosis generally and also defines principal diagnosis and comorbidity.

So, at the schema level, the path is PATIENTS.patient_id → DIAGNOSES.patient_id, then filter by the target patient and the past time period using valid_from/valid_to for historical state reconstruction, and optionally diagnosis_date for diagnoses dated within that period. The context includes a resolution_date column in DIAGNOSES, but it does not define how that column should be interpreted or used.

per_question.question: How have a patient's medications changed over time?
per_question.answer:
The retrieved context does not contain patient-level records, so it cannot determine how any specific patient’s medications changed over time.

What the context does provide is the schema-level way to trace medication history. The `MEDICATIONS` table stores patient medication prescriptions and administrations and is linked to a patient by `patient_id`. Relevant medication attributes are `medication_name`, `ndc_code`, `dosage`, `route`, `frequency`, `prescribing_provider_id`, `start_date`, and `end_date`. The full physical table also includes `is_deleted`, `valid_from`, `valid_to`, `created_at`, and `updated_at`.

From the glossary, `Historization` means time-bounded versions of records are kept using `valid_from` and `valid_to` so historical states can be reconstructed, and `Soft Delete` means a record may be marked inactive with `is_deleted` instead of being physically removed. Based on that context, medication changes over time would be identified by reviewing a patient’s `MEDICATIONS` records for the same `patient_id` across those effective-dating fields and timestamps. That would show when a medication was started or ended (`start_date`, `end_date`), and whether values such as `dosage`, `route`, `frequency`, `ndc_code`, or `prescribing_provider_id` changed between versions. `is_deleted` would indicate a logical removal rather than a physical deletion.

The glossary also defines `Medication Reconciliation` as comparing a patient’s medication orders with medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and interactions, but the retrieved context does not include a separate reconciliation table or a specific calculation/query procedure for it.

# Business Glossary - Advanced Healthcare Management System ## Patient Management **Patient** An individual receiving medical care, treatment, or services from the healthcare organization. Each patient has a unique medical record number and may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods. Patients can be identified by their medical record number (MRN), which is distinct from any external insurance identifiers. **Patient Registration** The process of capturing essential demographic and administrative information when a patient first presents to the healthcare system. This includes personal details, emergency contacts, insurance information, and consent forms. ## Clinical Concepts **Diagnosis** The identification of a disease, condition, or injury based on clinical evaluation, laboratory results, and diagnostic imaging. Diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification) for billing and epidemiological purposes. A patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses with varying levels of clinical priority. **Principal Diagnosis** The primary condition identified after study as chiefly responsible for admission of the patient to the healthcare facility. This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.
